claim.hypotheses_location: Introduction: paragraphs outlining predictions about EF and reward sensitivity (e.g., 'one possibility is that the mobilizing effects of reward incentives should be greater for lower-capacity individuals...' and predictions for NFC), preceding Section 2. Methods (approx. pp. 101–103).
claim.statement_location: Abstract: summary stating that lower executive function capacity and lower Need for Cognition predicted larger reward-induced increases in cognitive effort (reduced switch costs), whereas higher EF and higher NFC were less responsive to incentives.

data.source: Laboratory experiment with human participants performing a Stroop task and a task-switching paradigm; self-report questionnaires (Need for Cognition, BIS/BAS, Barratt Impulsiveness, GAD-7).
data.sample_size: 54 recruited; 7 excluded for <80% accuracy and 2 excluded for ≥15 missed deadlines; final N = 45 for main analyses; for NFC analyses, 2 additional participants excluded due to missing NFC responses (N = 43).
data.unit_of_analysis: Trial-level responses (RT and accuracy) from individual participants, with participant-level predictors (e.g., Stroop RT cost, NFC).
data.access_details: All raw data available via the Open Science Framework at http://osf.io/gqvsh.
data.notes: RT analyses on correct trials only; RTs log-transformed; practice effects modeled via a linear predictor of trial block; reward manipulated within-subjects (High vs Low); exclusions based on accuracy and missed deadlines; Stroop RT cost computed as standardized RT difference (incongruent − congruent).

method.description: Participants completed questionnaires (including Need for Cognition and BIS/BAS), then performed a computerized Stroop task to assess executive function capacity (via Stroop RT cost). Next, they completed two blocks of a task-switching paradigm in which reward magnitude for correct responses was manipulated within-subjects (High: 5¢; Low: 1¢). Switch costs (RT difference between switch and repeat trials) indexed cognitive effort. Mixed-effects regressions (R, lme4) assessed effects of reward, trial type, and individual differences (Stroop RT cost, NFC), with block as a predictor and BAS as a covariate in supplemental analyses.
steps: Recruit 54 participants from McGill participant pool/community; obtain consent (REB-approved).
Administer questionnaires: Need for Cognition (18 items), BIS/BAS; also Barratt Impulsiveness Scale and GAD-7 (standard lab battery).

Stroop task: practice, then 1 block of 120 trials (90 congruent, 30 incongruent); stimuli appear 500 ms after trial onset; response window 1.5 s; no feedback; responses via 'j','k','l' keys; colors: RGB (255,0,0), (0,255,0), (0,0,255).
Compute individual Stroop RT cost (standardized RT incongruent − congruent) as EF measure.
Task-switching paradigm: two counterbalanced blocks (High vs Low reward); 140 trials per block; stimulus onset 100 ms after trial onset; respond within 3 s; feedback provided.

Task-switching details: Red card => magnitude task (number >5?); Black card => parity task (odd/even); tasks cued by card color with zero cue–stimulus interval; equal number of parity and magnitude trials per block; numbers 2–9 excluding 5.
Reward manipulation: participants informed at block start whether correct responses pay 5¢ (High) or 1¢ (Low).
Between blocks: unrelated filler task and an 8-minute music break; then complete remaining reward condition block.
Exclusions: remove participants with <80% accuracy in either task or ≥15 missed deadlines in any block; for NFC analyses, exclude participants with missing NFC responses.

Statistical analysis: Mixed-effects regressions in R (lme4). RT models on correct trials with log-transformed RTs; accuracy via logistic mixed-effects. Predictors include trial type (switch vs repeat), reward condition, block (practice), and individual differences (Stroop RT cost or NFC), plus interactions; BAS included as covariate in supplemental models.
method.models: Mixed-effects regression models (lme4). RT models: linear mixed-effects on log RTs of correct trials; accuracy models: logistic mixed-effects. Fixed effects included trial type (switch vs repeat), reward (High vs Low), block (linear), and individual differences (Stroop RT cost or NFC), with their interactions (notably Trial type × Reward × Individual difference). Subject-level random effects included (details of random structure not specified).
method.outcome_variable: Primary: reaction time (log-transformed) and task switch cost (RT difference between switch and repeat trials). Secondary: response accuracy.
method.independent_variables: Reward condition (High vs Low), trial type (switch vs repeat), experimental block (practice), individual differences: Stroop RT cost (EF capacity), Need for Cognition (NFC).
method.control_variables: Experimental block (practice effect). Behavioral Activation Scale (BAS) as covariate in supplemental analyses. RT models restricted to correct trials.
method.tools_software: R programming language; lme4 package for mixed-effects regressions.

results.summary: Stroop showed robust interference: incongruent trials had longer RTs and lower accuracy. Task-switching showed typical switch costs (slower and less accurate on switch vs repeat). At the group level, reward did not significantly reduce switch costs. Critically, a significant three-way interaction indicated that lower EF capacity (larger Stroop RT cost) predicted greater reward-induced reductions in switch costs (increased effort), whereas higher EF individuals showed little to no reward responsivity. Accuracy did not show corresponding reward-related interactions. Need for Cognition also moderated reward effects: low-NFC individuals showed reward-induced switch cost reductions, while high-NFC individuals exhibited increases in switch costs under high reward. These effects were independent of reward sensitivity (BAS), which did not predict RTs, switch costs, or reward-induced changes.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Reward main effect on RT (High vs Low; β)
Three-way interaction: Trial type × Reward × Stroop RT cost (β)
Three-way interaction: Trial type × Reward × NFC (β)
Correlation: NFC vs Stroop RT cost (r)
Switch cost (Low Reward) = Switch RT − Repeat RT
Switch cost (High Reward) = Switch RT − Repeat RT

metadata.original_paper_title: Cognitive capacity limitations and Need for Cognition differentially predict reward-induced cognitive effort expenditure
